source_deck.deck_title: Determine Area of Triangles Lesson launch
source_deck.lesson_topic: Determine Area of Triangles Lesson launch

lesson_information.lesson_title: Determine Area of Triangles Lesson launch

learning_targets: I can determine the area of a triangle using a related rectangle or parallelogram.
i_can_statements: I can determine the area of a triangle using a related rectangle or parallelogram.

lesson_objective_and_student_success_cri.lesson_objective: determine the area of a triangle using a related rectangle or parallelogram.
student_success_criteria: I can identify the base and height.
I can explain why the triangle area formula works.
I can solve a source problem and justify my strategy.

preparation_notes: Queue the slide deck to Session 1 (slides 1-8).
Project the source visual(s) used in class: I can determine the area of a triangle using a related rectangle or parallelogram.
required_visuals: I can determine the area of a triangle using a related rectangle or parallelogram.
I can identify the base and height.
I can explain why the triangle area formula works.
How does the rectangle help you reason about triangle area?
Triangle A has base 10 units and height 6 units. What is its area?
Find the area of Triangle B with base 12 units and height 9 units.

focus_tasks: Estimate which shape has the greater area.
teacher_actions: Launch the actual opening from slides 1, 2: What shapes do you notice?
Collect notice-wonder thinking before naming the mathematical focus of the lesson.
student_actions: Complete the opening task and record an initial strategy, estimate, or observation.
Share one idea with a partner or the class using evidence from the source slide.
Respond to the notice-wonder prompt before transitioning into the main task.
evidence_of_learning: Students refer to the actual figure, prompt, or question from the source deck when sharing their first idea.
misconceptions_to_monitor: Watch for students choosing a slanted side instead of the perpendicular height.
Watch for missing square units or a formula applied to the wrong dimensions.
What misconception should we watch for?
embedded_supports: Offer sentence starters or a word bank while students explain their thinking.
Prompt students to rehearse an oral explanation with a partner before sharing aloud.

student_actions: Track the modeled reasoning and annotate the diagram, relationship, or formula shown in the source slides.
Explain how the source representation supports the strategy used in the lesson.
Respond to the reasoning prompt: I can identify the base and height.
evidence_of_learning: Students use the same decomposition, composition, formula, or measurement language shown in the source deck.
misconceptions_to_monitor: Watch for students choosing a slanted side instead of the perpendicular height.
Watch for missing square units or a formula applied to the wrong dimensions.

mini_lesson_modeling_concept_development.source_excerpt: Rectangle area review How does the rectangle help you reason about triangle area?

focus_tasks: Explain why doubling the triangle creates a parallelogram.
What misconception should we watch for?
Triangle A has base 10 units and height 6 units. What is its area?
A triangle has area 24 square units and height 6 units. What is the base?
teacher_actions: Coach the collaborative task from the source deck: Explain why doubling the triangle creates a parallelogram.
Pause for checks for understanding before students move into more independent work.
Use the source check question: What misconception should we watch for?
student_actions: Work with a partner or table group on the exact guided prompt from the slides.
Compare strategies and revise work when feedback or a classmate's idea changes the approach.
evidence_of_learning: Students explain why the strategy works, not just what answer they got.
Students respond to the source discussion or check-for-understanding prompt using complete reasoning.
misconceptions_to_monitor: Watch for students choosing a slanted side instead of the perpendicular height.
Watch for missing square units or a formula applied to the wrong dimensions.
What misconception should we watch for?
embedded_supports: Offer sentence starters or a word bank while students explain their thinking.
Use a brief small-group or low-distraction check-in for students who need extra processing time.
Prompt students to rehearse an oral explanation with a partner before sharing aloud.
guided_practice_collaborative_learning.source_excerpt: Triangle A has base 10 units and height 6 units. What is its area? Which measurement is the height?

focus_tasks: Find the area of Triangle B with base 12 units and height 9 units.
Show your work and label units.
teacher_actions: Assign the actual independent/application task: Find the area of Triangle B with base 12 units and height 9 units.
Circulate and confer while monitoring whether students can transfer the modeled reasoning independently.
student_actions: Solve the source task independently and show all reasoning needed to justify the answer.
Label dimensions, units, or key relationships exactly as the lesson expects.
evidence_of_learning: Students set up the problem correctly and maintain precision with labels and units.
misconceptions_to_monitor: Watch for students choosing a slanted side instead of the perpendicular height.
Watch for missing square units or a formula applied to the wrong dimensions.
What misconception should we watch for?
embedded_supports: Use a brief small-group or low-distraction check-in for students who need extra processing time.
Point out the key words in the problem statement before independent work begins.
independent_practice_application_station.source_excerpt: Find the area of Triangle B with base 12 units and height 9 units. Show your work and label units.

focus_tasks: What misconception should we watch for?
Triangle A has base 10 units and height 6 units. What is its area?
A triangle has area 24 square units and height 6 units. What is the base?
teacher_actions: Use the session close from the source slides: What misconception should we watch for?
Listen for precise mathematical language before dismissing the class or assigning follow-up work.
student_actions: Complete the closing check, summary, or exit-style prompt from the source deck.
Explain the strategy or conclusion using the lesson vocabulary.
evidence_of_learning: Students independently show whether they can apply the session's mathematical focus without teacher prompting.
misconceptions_to_monitor: Watch for students choosing a slanted side instead of the perpendicular height.
Watch for missing square units or a formula applied to the wrong dimensions.

embedded_supports: Offer sentence starters or a word bank while students explain their thinking.
